Q1: My study routine was pretty hands-on and practical. I'd block out about 19 hours a week, usually in longer sessions where I could really get into the material physically—working through problems, writing things out, sometimes pacing while I reviewed concepts aloud. I kept things consistent but flexible enough to accommodate my extracurriculars. I found that I learned best when I could actually do something with the material rather than just read about it.
Q2: I tried to keep regular times, mostly mornings and early afternoons when I had the energy to really focus. But honestly, my pattern would shift based on what else was going on—if I had activities or events, I'd adjust. Deadlines didn't stress me out too much because I stayed ahead of the curve generally. The consistency came more from habit than from strict scheduling.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check if there were new assignments posted or to see announcements from the instructor. Sometimes I'd go in specifically to download materials that I'd then work with offline. It wasn't something I did constantly—more like checking in periodically to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. I didn't feel the need to be on there all the time.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a central part of my routine—it was more of a tool I used when necessary. I'd grab what I needed and then do the actual learning elsewhere. I know some people live on those platforms, but for me it was just a means to an end. My routine was built around physical study spaces and hands-on work, not screen time.
Q5: The downloadable materials were the most useful to me—PDFs, lecture slides, anything I could print out and annotate by hand. I also found the assignment submission portal straightforward and reliable. Videos were helpful when I watched them, though I didn't always finish them in one sitting. Basically, anything that let me take the content and work with it in my own way felt valuable.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I'd post occasionally but it never felt like genuine learning to me—more like checking a box. I also didn't engage much with the optional quizzes or interactive elements because I had my own methods that worked better. It wasn't that they were bad; they just didn't fit how I learn best.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my understanding or my grade. If it helped me actually grasp a concept or practice applying it, I was in. Busy work or things that felt like they were just there to pad engagement metrics—those I could spot pretty quickly and I'd deprioritize them. I valued efficiency and genuine learning over just logging hours.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like suggestions rather than necessities. I'd evaluate them based on whether they'd actually help me, not just because they existed. Required activities had a different weight—I'd complete those regardless of how useful they seemed because they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I was more selective and honestly didn't feel guilty skipping things that didn't suit my learning style.
Q9: Absolutely, that's where most of my real studying happened. I'd download lecture materials, print them out, and work through them with a pen in hand. I used textbooks extensively and would often find supplementary resources online that explained things in ways that clicked better for me. My desk was covered in handwritten notes and printed diagrams—none of that shows up in any log.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper or reviewing my handwritten notes before an exam. I'd also study with peers in person, talking through concepts and quizzing each other. All of that deep learning happened completely offline. The LMS would just show that I'd logged in once to download something, but the real work was invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF, watch or read it once to get the gist, and then work with that material repeatedly offline. I'd pause, rewind mentally, take notes, and really process it away from the screen. One download might represent hours of actual engagement that happened later in my room or at the library with printed materials spread out around me.
Q12: There were moments mid-semester when my motivation dipped—I think around week six or seven when the novelty wore off but the end wasn't in sight yet. I was surprised by how much that affected me since I usually handle things well. My extracurriculars were also demanding around that time, so I had to really push through. The peer support intervention actually helped me reconnect with why I was doing this.
Q13: Medium stress was pretty constant for me, and competing responsibilities definitely shaped when and how I used the LMS. When I was fatigued, I'd just grab what I needed and get out rather than exploring or engaging deeply online. The platform became purely functional during busy periods—download, check deadlines, leave. I saved my mental energy for the actual studying.
Q14: Sure, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing while my mind was elsewhere. I'd be physically present on the platform but not really absorbing anything. Usually this happened when I was tired or trying to multitask. I learned to recognize when I was just going through the motions and would step away to come back fresh later.
Q15: I'd sometimes put off the discussion posts because they felt performative rather than educational. I knew they were part of participation, but I'd delay them until the last reasonable moment. Some readings that seemed tangential to the core material also got pushed back. It wasn't avoidance exactly—more like strategic prioritization based on what I knew would actually impact my learning and grades.
Q16: Deadlines were anchors for me but not stress triggers. I'd work backward from due dates to plan my study sessions, making sure I had enough time to engage properly with materials before submitting anything. The LMS deadline reminders were useful for keeping track, but I didn't cram or rush. My high attendance and exam scores came from staying ahead rather than reacting to pressure.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my approach. When I got positive feedback on an assignment, it confirmed my methods were working, so I'd continue with confidence. Constructive criticism made me adjust—I'd revisit those areas more carefully. But I didn't obsessively check for feedback; I trusted the process and knew my work was solid. The peer support also gave me informal feedback that was really valuable.
Q18: The course structure gave me clear milestones, which I appreciated. Knowing when things were due and what was expected helped me plan my independent study time effectively. However, the structure didn't force me to engage with the LMS constantly, which suited me fine. I could meet all the requirements while doing most of my actual learning in my own way, offline.
Q19: The flexibility to access materials and work independently made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when participation required specific online interactions that didn't match my learning style—like mandatory discussion posts that felt forced. The kinesthetic, hands-on approach I prefer isn't really supported by most LMS designs. I had to create my own engagement methods outside the platform.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might be clicking around, rewatching videos, reading and rereading, but not able to translate that into completed work. Or maybe they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's a sign of confusion or spinning wheels.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. I performed well—distinction level—with relatively low LMS engagement because my real learning happened offline. I'd download what I needed, process it through hands-on study methods, and come back only to submit work or check deadlines. The platform captured maybe 20% of my actual study effort. Efficiency and effective offline methods can make someone look disengaged when they're actually deeply invested.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent on pages. That's useful for identifying students who might be completely absent or struggling. But logs miss the quality of engagement, offline study, peer discussions, and whether someone is actually learning versus just present. They capture behavior, not understanding or effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely wrestling with the material, trying to understand it deeply, and connecting it to what I already know. It's not about time on a platform—it's about mental investment and effort toward mastery. For me, engagement looks like handwritten notes, practice problems, discussions with classmates, and that feeling when a concept finally clicks. It's active and personal, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline study habits, my peer study sessions, or the physical notes I created and reviewed repeatedly. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with classmates that clarified concepts or the time I spent applying knowledge practically. My low post-intervention mood and the surprise I felt at certain points in the course—that emotional journey is invisible too. The data shows a fraction of who I was as a student.
